title: Kim's Dangerous Liaisons: Putin's Pyongyang Visit and the New Northern Threat
content:
When Russian President Vladimir Putin traveled to Pyongyang in June 2024, his first visit to North Korea in nearly a quarter century, the optics were striking. Russian flags and portraits of Putin adorned the capital. He was treated to an elaborate welcome ceremony with a military honor guard and groups of balloon-toting children. This pageantry is a hallmark of North Korean politics, but the substance of the meeting was less anticipated.

The Treaty on Comprehensive Strategic Partnership the two men signed that day formalized a quietly growing relationship. Since Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, a military alliance has formed between these two nuclear-armed pariah states. By October 2024, around 11,000 North Korean troops had been deployed to support the Russian war effort. This great power cooperation is intended to pool resources when mobilization cannot match the scale of external threats.

A state must possess the will and focus to carry out such a consolidation strategy. This requires strong leaders who can force unpopular policies onto the agenda and manage inevitable crises. Consolidation takes time and serves as a period of intentional respite from costly foreign policy adventures. Both allies and enemies have a say in the strategy's success, as a consolidating state needs stability to rehabilitate its position.

The second Trump administration has pursued key elements of this consolidation strategy in response to the growing threat. The 2025 National Security Strategy explicitly identified the widening gap between the United States' means and ends. It proposed a program of national revitalization that rebalances external commitments. This approach makes generational domestic investments in core capabilities to increase U.S. power relative to its main rivals over time.

Similarly, the 2026 National Defense Strategy marks a historic shift in American military doctrine. Planners had the foresight to see that the country was fundamentally unprepared to face a new main threat. The strategy calls for a stronger focus on the Western Hemisphere and China, while reducing efforts in Europe. Ultimately, it outlines an ambitious program for mobilizing U.S. military-industrial resources.
